46: A direct revelation: my God, my God, why have you forsaken me? The cry of dereliction is the most difficult Christological moment in the Gospels — the eternal Son experiencing genuine God-forsakenness as the penalty of sin he is bearing for others. This is not merely the quoting of a Psalm for devotional comfort (though the Psalm ends in vindication); it is the actual experience of what Paul calls being made sin for us (2 Corinthians 5:21) and being cursed for us (Galatians 3:13). The darkness, the abandoned cry, and the torn curtain together form the Christological declaration: God was in Christ, in the place of the God-forsaken, reconciling the world to himself.
51: A fulfillment: the curtain of the temple was torn in two from top to bottom. The rending of the veil that separated the holy of holies from the holy place is the Christological climax of Matthew's temple-theology: the barrier between God and humanity that the entire Levitical system presupposed and maintained is destroyed by Christ's death. Access to the divine presence, which required the high priest alone once a year with blood, is now open to all — the way into the most holy place has been opened permanently through Christ's body (Hebrews 10:20). Matthew adds the opened tombs and the resurrection of the saints (Matthew 27:52-53) as the first-fruits of the general resurrection inaugurated by Christ's death.
1: A revelation of God: "When morning came, all the chief priests and the elders of the people took counsel against Jesus to put him to death." The early morning council — the formal Sanhedrin vote after the nighttime interrogation — is the Christological fulfillment of the passion predictions: Jesus is being handed over by the religious leadership. The "morning" is the deliberate timing of the legal formality; the nighttime session was the real decision. The Christological irony: the most religious people in Jerusalem are meeting at dawn to kill the Son of God.
2: A revelation of God: "And they bound him and led him away and delivered him over to Pilate the governor." The binding and delivery to Pilate is the fulfillment of Matthew 20:19 ("they will deliver him over to the Gentiles"). The passive "delivered over" (paredōkan) is the passion verb that runs through the entire narrative — from Judas delivering him to the chief priests to the chief priests delivering him to Pilate. The divine passive underlies each human delivery: the Son is being given over according to the Father's plan.
3: A direct fulfillment: "Then when Judas, his betrayer, saw that Jesus was condemned, he changed his mind and brought back the thirty pieces of silver to the chief priests and the elders." The change of mind (metamelomai, regret rather than metanoiō, repentance) is the Christological distinction: Judas feels regret but does not turn to the one he betrayed. The return of the silver is the Christological reversal of the transaction — but the reversal cannot undo the betrayal. The money returned does not buy back what was sold.
4: A direct fulfillment: "Saying, 'I have sinned by betraying innocent blood.' They said, 'What is that to us? See to it yourself.'" Judas' confession — "innocent blood" (haima athōon) — is the Christological testimony of the betrayer himself. Even the one who sold him knows him to be innocent. The chief priests' dismissal ("see to it yourself") is the Christological abandonment: the institution that paid for the betrayal refuses to receive the confession. The betrayer is left alone with his guilt.
5: A shadow: "And throwing down the pieces of silver into the temple, he departed, and he went and hanged himself." The suicide of Judas is the Christological contrast to Peter's response to his own failure: Peter wept bitterly and was restored; Judas despaired and died. The throwing of the silver into the temple is the final act of a man who cannot live with what he has done. The Christological bearing: the thirty pieces of silver thrown in the temple will fulfill Scripture (Matthew 27:9-10) even through the despair of the one who threw them.
6: A direct fulfillment: "But the chief priests, taking the pieces of silver, said, 'It is not lawful to put them into the treasury, since it is blood money.'" The irony of scrupulousness about blood money — by those who just used it to purchase an innocent man's death — is the Christological exposure of casuistry: they will not defile the treasury but have already defiled the Messiah. The same pattern as the oath-disputes of Matthew 23: meticulous about small rules while committing the largest violation.
7: A direct fulfillment: "So they took counsel and bought with them the potter's field as a burial place for strangers." The purchase of the potter's field with the blood money is the Christological fulfillment in motion: the exact use of the returned money fulfills what Zechariah prophesied. The "potter's field" becomes a permanent monument to the transaction — the field purchased with the price of the Son is still called "Field of Blood" (Matthew 27:8).
8: A direct fulfillment: "Therefore that field has been called the Field of Blood to this day." The naming of the field — permanent, public, remembered "to this day" — is the Christological monument to the betrayal. The land itself bears the testimony: this is the field that was purchased with the price of innocent blood. The topography of Jerusalem carries the evidence of what was done to the Son.
9: A direct fulfillment: "Then was fulfilled what had been spoken by the prophet Jeremiah, saying, 'And they took the thirty pieces of silver, the price of him on whom a price had been set by some of the sons of Israel.'" The fulfillment attribution to Jeremiah (with quotation from Zechariah 11:12-13, possibly combined with Jeremiah 19:1-13) is the Christological reading of the prophetic literature: the Son's betrayal price was named centuries before. The thirty pieces of silver as the slave price (Exodus 21:32) and the price of the rejected shepherd (Zech 11) are both fulfilled in the one transaction.
10: A direct fulfillment: "And they gave them for the potter's field, as the Lord directed me." The "as the Lord directed me" closes the prophetic citation with the divine agency behind the entire transaction. The Christological fulfillment: even the decision of the chief priests to purchase the potter's field with the blood money was "directed" by the Lord — the divine economy works through and despite the choices of human actors. The betrayal and its monetary consequence are within the Father's purpose.
11: A direct revelation: "Now Jesus stood before the governor, and the governor asked him, 'Are you the King of the Jews?' Jesus said, 'You have said so.'" The "you have said so" (sy legeis) before Pilate is the same formula as before Caiaphas (Matthew 26:64) and Judas (Matthew 26:25). The affirmation is genuine but turns the question back on the questioner: Pilate has identified the truth. The "King of the Jews" is a political title; the Christological reality exceeds it — he is the King of kings, whose throne is not in Jerusalem's political structures but in the eschatological kingdom.
12: A direct fulfillment: "But when he was accused by the chief priests and elders, he gave no answer." The silence of Jesus before accusation is the Christological fulfillment of Isaiah 53:7: "he opened not his mouth; like a lamb that is led to the slaughter." The one who spoke with authority in the temple, who silenced every questioner, who pronounced the seven woes — now stands in silence before the accusations of those he silenced. The silence is not defeat but the chosen posture of the one who submits to the Father's will.
13: A revelation of God: "Then Pilate said to him, 'Do you not hear how many things they testify against you?'" Pilate's astonishment at the silence — "do you not hear?" — is the Christological marvel of the governor who has never encountered someone who does not defend himself. The accusations are many; the defense is nothing. The Christological inversion: the one who has the most to say and the most grounds to say it chooses silence. The lamb before its shearers is silent.
14: A direct fulfillment: "But he gave him no answer, not even to a single charge, so that the governor was greatly amazed." Pilate's amazement at the total silence is the Christological effect of the suffering servant's posture. The governor who has seen every kind of accused person has never seen this: the innocent who does not protest. The amazement is the beginning of Pilate's private conviction of Jesus' innocence — which will be confirmed by his wife's dream (Matthew 27:19) and his own handwashing (Matthew 27:24).
15: A revelation of God: "Now at the feast the governor was accustomed to release for the crowd any one prisoner whom they wanted." The Passover custom of prisoner release is the Christological setting for the Barabbas exchange. The annual release of one prisoner at Passover frames the ultimate Passover exchange: the guilty is released, the innocent is condemned. The one who is the true Passover lamb (1 Corinthians 5:7) is rejected for the release of the criminal.
16: A revelation of God: "And they had then a notorious prisoner called Barabbas." Barabbas (bar-Abba, "son of the father") is the Christological contrast: the "son of the father" who is a murderer (Mark 15:7) is released while the true Son of the Father is condemned. The name itself is an ironic commentary. Barabbas represents every sinner released at the cost of the innocent — the substitutionary logic of the atonement enacted in the Passover drama of the courtyard.
17: A revelation of God: "So when they had gathered, Pilate said to them, 'Whom do you want me to release for you: Barabbas, or Jesus who is called Christ?'" Pilate's question — Barabbas or "Jesus who is called Christ" — is the Christological choice offered to every generation: the criminal or the Messiah. The crowd's choice of Barabbas is the choice of humanity for the guilty over the innocent, the known criminal over the unknown king. The one who is called Christ is the title that will be vindicated at the resurrection.
18: A revelation of God: "For he knew that it was out of envy that they had delivered him up." Pilate's perception — "out of envy" (dia phthonon) — is the Christological insight of an outsider who reads the situation more clearly than the insiders. The envy of the religious leaders toward Jesus (whose crowds, authority, and teaching they cannot match) is the political motivation behind the theological charge of blasphemy. The Christological irony: the pagan governor sees what the high priest refuses to see.
19: A revelation of God: "Besides, while he was sitting on the judgment seat, his wife sent word to him, 'Have nothing to do with that righteous man, for I have suffered much because of him today in a dream.'" Pilate's wife's dream warning — "that righteous man" (tō dikaiō ekeinō) — is the second Gentile testimony to Jesus' innocence (after Pilate's own perception in Matthew 27:18). The dream is the divine warning that Pilate will ignore. The Christological pattern: the Gentiles who have no prior relationship with Israel's Messiah recognize him more clearly than those who planned his death.
20: A revelation of God: "Now the chief priests and the elders persuaded the crowd to ask for Barabbas and destroy Jesus." The manipulation of the crowd by the chief priests is the Christological political machinery of the passion: the same crowd that cried "Hosanna to the Son of David" (Matthew 21:9) will now cry "crucify him" (Matthew 27:22-23) because the chief priests have worked on them. The crowd is not monolithic; it is the target of deliberate manipulation by those with power.
21: A revelation of God: "The governor again said to them, 'Which of the two do you want me to release for you?' And they said, 'Barabbas.'" The crowd's choice of Barabbas is the Christological rejection enacted publicly. The one whom the crowd asked about in Matthew 21:10 ("who is this?") and whom some identified as the prophet (Matthew 21:11) is now exchanged for a murderer. The Passover release that should set the captive free becomes the release of the guilty and the condemnation of the innocent.
22: A revelation of God: "Pilate said to them, 'Then what shall I do with Jesus who is called Christ?' They all said, 'Let him be crucified!'" The question "what shall I do with Jesus who is called Christ?" is the Christological question of history — the question every person and community must answer. The crowd's "crucify him!" is the specific answer of the passion, but the question is universal. The one who is "called Christ" is the one whose fate every generation determines by its response to him.
23: A revelation of God: "And he said, 'Why? What evil has he done?' But they shouted all the more, 'Let him be crucified!'" Pilate's question "what evil has he done?" (ti gar kakon epoiēsen) is the Christological declaration of innocence from the governor's own mouth. He finds no crime. The crowd's escalating demand — "all the more" — is the Christological dynamic of mob hatred: the declaration of innocence intensifies the demand for execution. The justice of the system is overwhelmed by the passion of the crowd.
24: A revelation of God: "So when Pilate saw that he was gaining nothing, but rather that a riot was beginning, he took water and washed his hands before the crowd, saying, 'I am innocent of this man's blood; see to it yourselves.'" Pilate's handwashing is the Christological gesture of transferred responsibility — but it does not transfer the guilt. The one who delivers an innocent man to execution is responsible regardless of what his hands do afterward. The Christological bearing: the handwashing ritual echoes Deuteronomy 21:6-9 (the elders' handwashing at the site of an unsolved murder). Pilate enacts the ritual; the guilt remains.
25: A revelation of God: "And all the people answered, 'His blood be on us and on our children!'" The "his blood be on us" is the covenantal liability formula — the acceptance of responsibility for a death (cf. 2 Samuel 1:16; Matthew 3:28-29). This is not a racial curse on all Jews for all time (a misreading that has fueled terrible violence) but the Jerusalem crowd's acceptance of liability for a specific act. The Christological bearing: the blood they claim responsibility for is the blood that "speaks better things than Abel" (Hebrews 12:24) — the blood that accomplishes forgiveness for those who call on it.
26: A revelation of God: "Then he released for them Barabbas, and having scourged Jesus, delivered him to be crucified." The scourging before crucifixion is the Christological fulfillment of Isaiah 53:5: "by his wounds we are healed." The Roman scourging was brutal — a leather whip with bone or metal fragments that tore the flesh. The one who "bore our griefs and carried our sorrows" (Isaiah 53:4) receives the physical punishment in his own body. The delivery to crucifixion is the final human "delivering over" of the Son.
27: A revelation of God: "Then the soldiers of the governor took Jesus into the governor's headquarters, and they gathered the whole battalion before him." The gathering of the whole battalion — six hundred soldiers — for the mocking of one prisoner is the Christological excess of cruelty: an entire regiment organized to mock a condemned man. The Christological bearing: the full weight of Rome's military power is directed at the one who refused to call twelve legions of angels (Matthew 26:53). The one who could have commanded the armies of heaven submits to the mockery of one earthly battalion.
28: A revelation of God: "And they stripped him and put a scarlet robe on him." The stripping — the vulnerability of nakedness — is the Christological exposure of the one who "emptied himself" (Philippians 2:7) to the extreme. The scarlet robe (the soldier's military cloak) is the mock-royal garment: they dress the "king" in military red, making his royalty a joke. The Christological irony: the one who will be clothed in white at the resurrection is dressed in military scarlet for his mock coronation.
29: A revelation of God: "And twisting together a crown of thorns, they put it on his head and put a reed in his right hand. And kneeling before him, they mocked him, saying, 'Hail, King of the Jews!'" The crown of thorns and the reed scepter are the Christological parody of kingship. The thorns recall the curse of Genesis 3:18 ("thorns and thistles the ground shall produce for you") — the Son who bears the curse wears its symbol as his crown. The kneeling and "Hail, King of the Jews!" is the mock homage that will one day be real: "every knee shall bow" (Philippians 2:10), but now in mockery.
30: A revelation of God: "And they spit on him and took the reed and struck him on the head." The spitting and striking — the second instance of this treatment (after Matthew 26:67) — is the Christological fulfillment of Isaiah 50:6: "I hid not my face from disgrace and spitting." The suffering servant who endures the full humiliation is the Son who chose not to be spared it. The reed used as a mock scepter becomes the instrument of beating — the symbols of kingship turned against the king.
31: A revelation of God: "And when they had mocked him, they stripped him of the robe and put his own clothes on him and led him away to crucify him." The removal of the mock-royal robe and the restoration of his own clothes marks the transition from mockery to execution. The Christological movement: the mock enthronement is over; the real enthronement — on the cross, with the title "King of the Jews" (Matthew 27:37) nailed above him — is beginning. The cross is the throne that the soldiers' mockery inadvertently rehearsed.
32: A revelation of God: "As they went out, they found a man of Cyrene, Simon by name. They compelled this man to carry his cross." Simon of Cyrene — a Diaspora Jew from North Africa, possibly a Passover pilgrim — is compelled to carry the cross that Jesus can no longer carry alone. The Christological bearing: the one who called disciples to "take up your cross" (Matthew 16:24) is himself unable to carry his. Simon carrying the cross behind Jesus is the literal enactment of the disciple's call. The first disciple of the passion is compelled rather than invited.
33: A revelation of God: "And when they came to a place called Golgotha (which means Place of a Skull)." The Place of a Skull — Golgotha in Aramaic, Calvaria in Latin — is the location outside the city walls where executions occurred. The Christological geography: "outside the gate" (Hebrews 13:12) is the place of the excluded, the unclean, those outside the covenant's protected space. The Son of God is crucified outside the holy city, in the place of the cursed. "Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree" (Galatians 3:13; Deuteronomy 21:23) — and he bears the curse.
34: A revelation of God: "They offered him wine to drink, mixed with gall, but when he tasted it, he would not drink it." The refusal of the wine mixed with gall is the Christological choice to face the cross with full consciousness rather than with the pain-dulling potion offered. The Christological bearing: the cup he must drink is the cup of divine wrath (Matthew 26:39), not the cup of narcotic mercy. The one who prayed "not my will but yours" in Gethsemane faces the cross with unclouded awareness of what he is doing and why.
35: A direct fulfillment: "And when they had crucified him, they divided his garments among them by casting lots." The casting of lots for the garments is the fulfillment of Psalms 22:18: "they divide my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots." The crucifixion has not yet been described — it is passed over in a single subordinate clause — and immediately Psalm 22 begins to be fulfilled in detail. The Christological use of Ps 22 as the interpretive framework for the crucifixion: the psalm of abandonment is simultaneously the psalm of praise and ultimate vindication.
36: A revelation of God: "Then they sat down and kept watch over him there." The soldiers sitting and watching over the crucified prisoner is the Christological vigil of those who do not understand what they are witnessing. They watch to prevent rescue; they are unaware that they are watching the central event of human history. The Christological patience of the cross: the Son hangs and is watched by those who put him there, who mock him (Matthew 27:40-44), while the Father's plan proceeds around their watchfulness.
37: A direct revelation: "And over his head they put the charge against him, which read, 'This is Jesus, the King of the Jews.'" The titulus — the public charge posted on the cross — is the Christological proclamation nailed above the crucified one. The charge intended as mockery is the truth: he is the King of the Jews. In all four Gospels this inscription is preserved, the one thing the Roman execution documented about the man being killed. The cross becomes the throne on which the title is displayed: "this is Jesus, the King of the Jews."
38: A shadow: "Then two robbers were crucified with him, one on the right and one on the left." The two robbers — one on the right, one on the left — is the Christological fulfillment of Isaiah 53:12: "he was numbered with the transgressors." The positions requested by James and John's mother (Matthew 20:21: "one at your right, one at your left in your kingdom") are occupied by criminals. The places of honor at the cross are given to sinners — which is the Christological principle of the entire passion: he takes the place of sinners.
39: A direct fulfillment: "And those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads." The head-wagging of the passersby is the fulfillment of Psalms 22:7: "all who see me mock me; they make mouths at me; they wag their heads." The suffering of the cross is not merely physical but the complete social rejection that Psalm 22 describes: the public spectacle of mockery, the abandonment, the taunting. The Christological weight: every detail of the mockery is the lived experience of what the Psalm prophesied.
40: A revelation of God: "And saying, 'You who would destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days, save yourself! If you are the Son of God, come down from the cross.'" The mock-challenge "if you are the Son of God, come down" echoes the wilderness temptation (Matthew 4:3: "if you are the Son of God, command these stones") — the same "if" of satanic testing, the same invitation to prove divine sonship through spectacular rescue. The Christological response to both is identical: he does not come down, does not turn stones to bread, does not prove himself on the tempter's terms. The Son of God is proven precisely by staying on the cross.
41: A revelation of God: "So also the chief priests, with the scribes and elders, mocked him, saying..." The institutional mockery — chief priests, scribes, elders — is the official religious establishment of Jerusalem deriding the condemned man. Those who condemned him for blasphemy now speak the most ironic truth of the Gospel in their mockery.
42: A revelation of God: "'He saved others; he himself he cannot save. He is the King of Israel; let him come down now from the cross, and we will believe in him.'" The Christological irony at its peak: "he saved others; himself he cannot save." They mean it as a taunt; it is the deepest theological truth of the atonement. He cannot save himself precisely because he is saving others. The exchange — his death for their life — is the mechanism the mockers name without understanding. The condition "come down and we will believe" is the reversed condition: it is by staying on the cross that he accomplishes what makes belief possible.
43: A direct fulfillment: "He trusts in God; let God deliver him now, if he desires him. For he said, 'I am the Son of God.'" The taunt "let God deliver him now, if he desires him" is the fulfillment of Psalms 22:8: "He trusts in the LORD; let him deliver him; let him rescue him, for he delights in him!" The Psalm's mockery is quoted by the real mockers. The Christological bearing: the Father will deliver him — on the third day, not from the cross. The delivery comes through death, not instead of it.
44: A revelation of God: "And the robbers who were crucified with him also reviled him in the same way." The robbers' mockery (contrasted with Luke 23:40-43 where one repents) is the Christological context of complete abandonment: not one person at Calvary defends or comforts him. He is mocked by the passersby, the religious leaders, and even the criminals crucified beside him. The total isolation of the cross — the full weight of human rejection — is the Christological condition under which the atoning work is done.
45: A revelation of God: "Now from the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land until the ninth hour." The three-hour darkness (noon to 3 PM) is the Christological cosmic sign: the sun withholds its light over the crucifixion. The darkness echoes the Exodus plague of darkness (Exodus 10:21-23), the prophetic darkness of the Day of the LORD (Amos 8:9: "I will make the sun go down at noon and darken the earth in broad daylight"), and the primordial chaos before creation (Genesis 1:2). The cosmos responds to the death of the one who is the light of the world (John 8:12).
47: A revelation of God: "And some of the bystanders, hearing it, said, 'This man is calling Elijah.'" The misunderstanding of "Eli, Eli" (Aramaic for "my God") as a call to Elijah is the Christological darkening of comprehension at the cross: those closest to the event cannot hear what is being spoken. The one who cried out the opening of Psalm 22 is misheard as calling for Elijah (the prophet expected before the Day of the LORD). The bystanders are near the most significant speech in human history and cannot understand it.
48: A revelation of God: "And one of them at once ran and took a sponge, filled it with sour wine, and put it on a reed and gave it to him to drink." The sour wine (oxos) offered on a reed is the fulfillment of Psalms 69:21: "for my thirst they gave me sour wine to drink." The act may be a mercy (quenching the dying man's thirst) or part of the mockery (keeping him alive to see if Elijah comes). The Christological ambiguity: at the cross, mercy and mockery are intertwined, but the Psalm is being fulfilled either way.
49: A revelation of God: "But the others said, 'Wait, let us see whether Elijah will come to save him.'" The waiting to see if Elijah comes is the Christological exposure of the crowd's theological framework: they expect miraculous intervention, the coming of Elijah to rescue the righteous. The intervention that is coming is not Elijah but the resurrection — and it will not come to interrupt the cross but to vindicate the one who completed it. The crowd waits for the wrong rescue.
50: A direct revelation: "And Jesus cried out again with a loud voice and yielded up his spirit." The loud cry before death — "yielded up his spirit" (aphēken to pneuma) — is the Christological act of voluntary death. The verb "yielded up" (or "released") is the active voice of giving: he gives up his spirit, he does not have it taken from him. The one who said "no one takes my life from me, I lay it down of my own accord" (John 10:18) does exactly that. The death is the supreme act of Christological self-giving.
52: A direct revelation: "The tombs also were opened. And many bodies of the saints who had fallen asleep were raised." The resurrection of the saints at the moment of Jesus' death is the Christological firstfruits of the general resurrection: the cross accomplishes the breakthrough of death's power that the resurrection will fully manifest. The risen saints who appear in Jerusalem (Matthew 27:53) are the preview of what the general resurrection will bring. The one who said "I am the resurrection and the life" (John 11:25) opens tombs by his own death.
53: A revelation of God: "And coming out of the tombs after his resurrection they went into the holy city and appeared to many." The appearance of the risen saints "after his resurrection" is the Christological ordering: the saints' resurrection is contingent on and follows from Christ's. "After his resurrection" is Matthew's clarification that Christ's resurrection is the cause and the saints' is the effect. 1 Corinthians 15:20 calls Christ "the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep" — these risen saints are the first harvest of the firstfruits.
54: A direct revelation: "When the centurion and those who were with him, keeping watch over Jesus, saw the earthquake and what took place, they were filled with awe and said, 'Truly this was the Son of God!'" The centurion's confession — "truly this was the Son of God" — is the Christological declaration that frames Matthew's Gospel: the voice from heaven at the baptism (Matthew 3:17: "this is my beloved Son"), the Transfiguration voice (Matthew 17:5), the disciples' confession (Matthew 16:16), and now a Roman soldier at the crucifixion. The Son of God is confessed at the moment of death by a Gentile soldier — the universal scope of the confession is beginning.
55: A revelation of God: "There were also many women there, looking on from a distance, who had followed Jesus from Galilee, ministering to him." The women who watch from a distance — those who ministered to him from Galilee — are the Christological witnesses who remain when the disciples have fled. The same verb "follow" (akoloutheō) used for discipleship throughout the Gospel identifies these women as genuine disciples. They are the ones who will be the first witnesses to the resurrection (Matthew 28:1-10). The passion's witnesses are the resurrection's first witnesses.
56: A revelation of God: "Among whom were Mary Magdalene and Mary the mother of James and Joseph and the mother of the sons of Zebedee." The named women — Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James and Joseph, Salome the mother of the Zebedee sons — are the Christological cloud of witnesses at the cross. Salome, who asked for her sons' places at Jesus' right and left (Matthew 20:20-21), now watches from a distance as those places are occupied by criminals. The answer to her request has been given.
57: A direct fulfillment: "When it was evening, there came a rich man from Arimathea, named Joseph, who also was a disciple of Jesus." The rich man Joseph of Arimathea as the one who provides the tomb is the fulfillment of Isaiah 53:9: "they made his grave with the wicked and with a rich man in his death." The Christological precision: even the burial arrangements fulfill the prophetic details. A disciple who had been hidden (John 19:38: "secretly for fear of the Jews") comes forward at the moment of death to honor the one he had followed privately.
58: A revelation of God: "He went to Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus. Then Pilate ordered it to be given to him." The request for the body — and Pilate's grant — is the Christological provision for the burial that fulfills Scripture (Isaiah 53:9). The same Pilate who found Jesus innocent but delivered him to death now grants the body to a disciple for burial. The Christological grace: even the mechanisms of the Roman system are ordered toward the proper burial of the Son.
59: A revelation of God: "And Joseph took the body and wrapped it in a clean linen shroud." The clean linen shroud is the Christological honor given to the body of the one who was stripped, mocked, and executed. The burial preparation is the act of dignity given to the degraded — the washing and wrapping of the body that will be left in the tomb for three days and then vacated. The empty grave clothes will be the resurrection's first visible sign (John 20:6-7).
60: A revelation of God: "And laid it in his own new tomb, which he had cut in the rock. And he rolled a great stone to the entrance of the tomb and went away." The new tomb — previously unoccupied — is the Christological provision for the burial of the one who "had nowhere to lay his head" (Matthew 8:20). He who had no permanent home in life is given a new tomb in death — and will vacate it in three days. The great stone rolled to the entrance will be rolled away by the angel (Matthew 28:2) to show that the tomb is empty.
61: A revelation of God: "Mary Magdalene and the other Mary were there, sitting opposite the tomb." The women's vigil — sitting opposite the tomb — is the Christological continuity of witness: those who watched the crucifixion (Matthew 27:55-56) now watch the burial. They know where the body is. They will be the first to come to the tomb on Sunday morning (Matthew 28:1). The Christological witness chain: from crucifixion to burial to empty tomb, the women are the consistent witnesses.
62: A revelation of God: "The next day, that is, after the Day of Preparation, the chief priests and the Pharisees gathered before Pilate." The Sanhedrin's visit to Pilate on the Sabbath — the day after the crucifixion — is the Christological irony of the passion's aftermath. Those who insisted on death are now worried about the resurrection prediction. The urgency of their visit violates the Sabbath they insisted Jesus violated.
63: A direct revelation: "Saying, 'Sir, we remember how that impostor said, while he was still alive, 'After three days I will rise.''" The chief priests remember the resurrection prediction better than the disciples do. The Christological irony: they call him "that deceiver" (planos, imposter) while quoting his prediction accurately. The prediction they memorized as a threat becomes the announcement of what is about to happen — despite, and through, their efforts to prevent it.
64: A revelation of God: "Therefore order the tomb to be made secure until the third day, lest his disciples go and steal him away and tell the people, 'He has risen from the dead,' and the last fraud will be worse than the first." The securing of the tomb is the Christological last line of defense against the resurrection — and it will fail. The very measures taken to prevent the resurrection become the evidence for it: the sealed, guarded tomb that is found empty on the third day cannot be explained by theft.
65: A revelation of God: "Pilate said to them, 'You have a guard of soldiers. Go, make it as secure as you can.'" Pilate's grant of the guard is the Christological provision of human security against divine action. "Make it as secure as you can" — all the security of Rome placed against the power of the Father who raised the Son. The tomb that is made "as secure as possible" will be the tomb from which the Son rises, demonstrating that no human security can contain him.
66: A revelation of God: "So they went and made the tomb secure by sealing the stone and setting a guard." The sealed stone and the posted guard are the final human measures against the resurrection. The Christological contrast: the sealed tomb and the guards become the conditions under which the resurrection is most publicly demonstrated. The empty sealed tomb with the guards fallen like dead men (Matthew 28:4) is the most secure evidence that what happened cannot be explained by human activity.
